_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Cardiovascular: Adult and Pediatric Syncope and Near Syncope. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
condition: Adult and Pediatric Syncope and Near Syncope
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Cardiovascular: Adult and Pediatric Syncope and Near Syncope

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Cardiovascular: Adult and Pediatric Syncope and Near Syncope
points: Transfer for further evaluation 1. Syncope is heralded by both the loss of consciousness and the loss of postural tone and resolves spontaneously without medical interventions. Syncope typically is abrupt in onset and resolves equally quickly. EMS clinicians may find the patient awake and alert on initial evaluation

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Abrupt loss of consciousness with loss of postural tone
Patients with alternate and obvious cause of loss of consciousness (e.g., trauma ' See Head Injury Guideline)
Patients with ongoing mental status changes or coma should be treated per the Altered Mental Status Guideline
Patients with persistent new neurologic deficit [See Suspected Stroke/Transient Ischemic Attack Guideline]

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Patients suffering from syncope due to arrhythmia may experience recurrent arrhythmias and should therefore be placed on a cardiac monitor
Geriatric patients suffering falls from standing may sustain significant injury and should be diligently screened for trauma. [General Trauma Management Guideline]

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: By being most proximate to the scene and to the patient's presentation, EMS clinicians are commonly in a unique position to identify the cause of syncope. Consideration of potential causes, ongoing monitoring of vitals and cardiac rhythm and detailed exam and history are essential pieces of information to pass on to hospital clinicians
For patients where a lower risk etiology is suspected, e.g., vasovagal syncope, decisions regarding delayed or non-transport should be made in consultation with medical direction
High-risk causes of syncope include, but are not limited to, the following: a. Cardiovascular i. Myocardial infarction
Consider high-risk 12-lead EKG features including, but not limited to:. a. Evidence of QT prolongation (generally over 500 msec)
Evidence of alternate etiology, including seizure
Evidence of cardiac dysfunction (e.g., evidence of congestive heart failure (CHF), arrhythmia).

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Cardiac rhythm present when patient is symptomatic
' Patient status on EMS arrival: recovered or still symptomatic

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
